items.q: How is a dynamic equilibrium different from a static one?
items.correct: In a dynamic equilibrium, forward and reverse reactions continue at equal rates; in a static equilibrium, nothing is happening at the particle level.
items.misconception: Dynamic and static equilibria are the same thing because both show constant macroscopic properties.
items.random_wrong: A dynamic equilibrium means the system is still changing visibly.
items.why: Macroscopically both look identical (no observable change), so students don't grasp the crucial particle-level distinction.

items.q: What state are the ions in when defining lattice enthalpy of formation?
items.correct: Gaseous ions — lattice enthalpy is the energy change when one mole of solid ionic compound forms from its constituent ions in the GAS phase.
items.misconception: The ions are in aqueous solution before forming the lattice.
items.random_wrong: The ions start as solid elements before being assembled into the lattice.
items.why: Students confuse lattice enthalpy with enthalpy of solution; they don't realise the Born-Haber cycle requires gas-phase ions as the reference point.

items.q: When an acid neutralises an alkali, what is the pH of the resulting solution?
items.correct: Approximately 7 if equivalent amounts of strong acid and strong alkali react — but it varies with strength.
items.misconception: Neutralisation always produces a solution with pH exactly 7 regardless of the acid and base used.
items.random_wrong: Neutralisation always produces a solution with pH 0.
items.why: Students learn the rule 'neutral = 7' so rigidly that they ignore weak-acid/weak-base cases where the salt is acidic or basic.

items.q: An alkane has 8 carbons. How many hydrogens? (CₙH₂ₙ₊₂)
items.why: The real figure is 18 H atoms. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
